text:
Powered byOutside

SANTIAGO, CHILE - OCTOBER 27: Colby Lange and Grant Koontz of Team United States pose on the podium of Track Cycling - Men's Madison at Parque Peñalolen on Day 7 of Santiago 2023 Pan Am Games on October 27, 2023 in Santiago, Chile. (Photo by Buda Mendes/Getty Images)(Photo: Buda Mendes/Getty Images)

If there is one thing you must know aboutGrant Koontzit’s that he really loves cycling. That singular fact, or relationship if you consider it in a slightly different context, explains a lot.



It’s a love story that has brought him from the road, to gravel, to the track, as a journeyman looking for a career to make the love story last just a little bit longer. It’s taken him from nightly dinners of eggs and sweet potatoes in a pop-up RV parked illegally on the streets of Boulder, to a bikepacking trip to theMidsouth gravel raceon the eve of the COVID-19 pandemic, and then, pivotally, to the Eurovision-esque glitz and pageantry of the made-for-TV Track Champions League.

Ultimately that passion has delivered the 30-year-old to theParis Olympicsas Team USA’s sole male track representative, even if it has been a wildly roundabout way to get there.

“In a sense, I was a lost puppy,“ Koontz said a couple of days before the Games began of his journey from gravel vagabond to the Olympic prep camp in Belgium. “Three years ago I was watching the Olympics from the sideline as the track world today shut down during the pandemic. I was still very much a novice. I knew I had the barebones tools to be a good track racer but I hadn’t had a chance to excel.

“Obviously, I wanted to make it to Paris but I needed to start out taking baby steps forward. I guess that’s always been the paralyzing thing for me, just the distractions elsewhere. Things like gravel became a distraction because it was really about having fun and staying motivated through the pandemic, but I knew, and my wife really convinced me, that if I could get to focus on one thing I would be super good at it.

“When I was finally able to do that and be good enough to turn my back on other interesting opportunities was what brought me success. The focus had to be falling in love with the process and not only relying on the love of cycling that has been going on for 12 years.”

While those sentiments and the journey Koontz has been on do fit the beats of other athletes’ dragged-out battles against luck, competition, and motivation to make it to the Olympic level, Koontz had the added challenge that comes from the United States’ complicated relationship with track cycling.

In the early 1900s, track cycling in the United States was huge. Figures likeMajor Taylorwere international stars, while fans packed famous venues like Madison Square Garden in New York City to watch not basketball or hockey, but track events. In fact, throughout the first half of the 20th century European professionals came to the United States to race track events for extra cash and exposure.

Now, track cycling is but a shadow of its past. Velodromes are sparse, with only three indoor tracks among the 22 total tracks currently in use in the U.S., which puts the U.S. on the back foot in international competition and development.

There have been exceptions to the rule — most notablyJennifer Valente’s gold medal at the omnium in the Tokyo Olympics and the women’s team pursuit silver medals in London and Rio, bronze in Tokyo, and nowgold in Paris— but on the men’s side, success in major championships has been below par as evidenced by their inability to qualify a team pursuit for the past two Olympic cycles despite significant investment into that cause.

“I have shifted my focus a few times,” Koontz said of his time in the Olympic pipeline with USA Cycling’s track program. “The initial plan was for us to qualify in the team pursuit, but once it was clear that the writing was on the wall that we weren’t going to qualify for the team pursuit I switched my focus to the Madison which is what I thought we’d have a better chance of getting a spot.

“I honestly hadn’t focused a lot on the omnium until this year because I thought my best shot at the Olympics was in literally any other event. But ultimately my focus on all the other events has helped me be a well-rounded rider for the omnium.”

Nevertheless, qualifying questions, funding concerns, and the uncertainty of selection are now behind Koontz. Instead, he is now facing the purity of the racing and faces the ultimate test of endurance track racers: the omnium.

The omnium is a one-day event consisting of four races: a scratch race, a tempo race, an eliminator, and the all-important points race. While each of these races has its own intricacies, when it comes down to brass-tax the demands of the races are similar to the technical challenges the rest of cycling faces. More and more, the big variable beyond raw watts is the aerodynamics of the rider.

For Koontz, a self-described cycling romantic, the technical challenges might not seem like they would be of much interest. But ever since he was selected to the team earlier this summer Koontz has delved into the tech challenges that come with racing at the tippy-top of cycling performance.

“Two weeks ago I did a bunch of on-bike on-track testing,” Koontz said. “Obviously, that means everything has to be taken with a grain of salt because outside forces can affect it, but still it is pivotal to see what helmet to use, what gloves to use, what shoe covers to use — everything.”

With eyes towards optimizing everything, hard choices had to be made, including skipping over a bike built for the American track program with the express purpose of having an advantage at these Paris Olympics.

“Look just released a new P24 track bikeand it just didn’t work for me,” Koontz said. “It is not adjustable enough for me and my position so we went back to the (Look) T20 bike because, at the end of the day, the bike is only as good as your position. You can save way more on helmets and shoe covers and those things rather than just riding all the cool new shit.”

Yet on August 8, the deck gets reset again. All the riders from around the world who will compete for medals over the frantic four events will have to forget about the aero advantages they may or may not have and simply race. At the end of the day, whatever way one splices it, racing is always more of an art than it is a science. The omnium is no different, especially since its biggest point haul comes in the race where surprises can turn up the established order.

“Consistency is key,” Koontz said. “You don’t have to be the best in the world at all of the events, it’s just about consistency over the first three races and just placing in the money, because the points race is the highest weighted event for those overall points, and that’s really what the training has been geared towards. If you can’t points race, you can’t be the omnium winner.”

To learn more about Koontz and his journey, be sure to check out hisInstagram (@rakoooontz) where he has been sharing details and journal entries from the pivotal points over the last few years. It is well worth the read!
title: Grant Koontz's Long Journey to Becoming an Olympic Track Cyclist
author: Logan Jones-Wilkins
description: The 30-year-old is the lone American male in Olympic track racing this year. How he reached this position is the culmination of a years-long love affair with cycling in all its forms.
